study.journal: Rosén et al. Awake prone positioning in patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19: the PROFLO multicenter randomized clinical trial. Crit Care. 2021.
study.study_type: Prospective multicenter open-label randomized clinical trial

clinical_question.condition: Hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19

population_context.population_description: Adult patients with confirmed COVID-19 and moderate to severe hypoxemic respiratory failure receiving high-flow nasal oxygen or noninvasive ventilation with PaO2/FiO2 ratio ≤20 kPa.
population_context.risk_group: COVID-19 patients with moderate to severe hypoxemic respiratory failure on HFNO or NIV
population_context.exclusions: Oxygen supplementation with device other than HFNO or NIV; inability to assume prone position; immediate need for intubation; severe hemodynamic instability; previous intubation for COVID-19; pregnancy; terminal illness; do-not-intubate order; inability to understand study information

intervention.name: Awake prone positioning protocol
intervention.parameter_values: Targeting at least 16 hours of awake prone positioning per day
intervention.timing: Initiated after randomization in non-intubated patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure

comparator.description: Standard care without protocolized awake prone positioning; APP could be prescribed at clinician discretion

strengths: Adequate blinded interim analysis and futility assessment
limitations: Small sample size (75 patients) relative to original planned sample (240 patients)
Open-label design with awareness of allocation
Rapidly declining case numbers in January 2021 led to earlier interim analysis
Limited generalizability due to enrollment from only three Swedish hospitals
No correction for multiple testing in secondary endpoint analyses
adverse_events: Pressure sores: 9 patients (23%) in control group vs 2 patients (6%) in prone group (difference -18%, 95% CI -33% to -2%, P=0.032)

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to adult COVID-19 patients with moderate to severe hypoxemic respiratory failure supported by HFNO or NIV in hospital settings. Results from Swedish tertiary and county hospitals may have limited generalizability to other healthcare systems and populations.

quoted_sentences.design: We conducted a multicenter randomized clinical trial. Adult patients with confirmed COVID-19, high-flow nasal oxygen or noninvasive ventilation for respiratory support and a PaO2/FiO2 ratio ≤20 kPa were randomly assigned to a protocol targeting 16 h prone positioning per day or standard care.
quoted_sentences.intervention: A protocol targeting at least 16 h APP per day was initiated. Prone and semi-prone positioning was allowed. Flat supine positioning was discouraged and patients were instructed to place themselves in the semi-recumbent or lateral position in between proning sessions.
quoted_sentences.results: Within 30 days after enrollment, 13 patients (33%) were intubated in the control group versus 12 patients (33%) in the prone group (HR 1.01 (95% CI 0.46–2.21), P=0.99).
quoted_sentences.outcome: The implemented protocol for awake prone positioning increased duration of prone positioning, but did not reduce the rate of intubation in patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19 compared to standard care.

_raw_text:
Rosén et al. Crit Care (2021) 25:209
https://doi.org/10.1186/s13054-021-03602-9
RESEARCH Open Access
Awake prone positioning in patients
with hypoxemic respiratory failure due
to COVID-19: the PROFLO multicenter
randomized clinical trial
Jacob Rosén1*† , Erik von Oelreich2,3†, Diddi Fors1, Malin Jonsson Fagerlund2,3, Knut Taxbro4, Paul Skorup5,
Ludvig Eby6, Francesca Campoccia Jalde2,7, Niclas Johansson8,9, Gustav Bergström5 , Peter Frykholm1 and the
PROFLO Study Group
Abstract
Background: The effect of awake prone positioning on intubation rates is not established.The aim of this trial was
to investigate if a protocol for awake prone positioning reduces the rate of endotracheal intubation compared with
standard care among patients with moderate to severe hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19.
Methods: We conducted a multicenter randomized clinical trial. Adult patients with confirmed COVID-19, high-
flow nasal oxygen or noninvasive ventilation for respiratory support and a PaO /FiO ratio 20 kPa were randomly
2 2 ≤
assigned to a protocol targeting 16 h prone positioning per day or standard care. The primary endpoint was intuba-
tion within 30 days. Secondary endpoints included duration of awake prone positioning, 30-day mortality, ventilator-
free days, hospital and intensive care unit length of stay, use of noninvasive ventilation, organ support and adverse
events. The trial was terminated early due to futility.
Results: Of 141 patients assessed for eligibility, 75 were randomized of whom 39 were allocated to the control group
and 36 to the prone group. Within 30 days after enrollment, 13 patients (33%) were intubated in the control group
versus 12 patients (33%) in the prone group (HR 1.01 (95% CI 0.46–2.21), P 0.99). Median prone duration was 3.4 h
=
[IQR 1.8–8.4] in the control group compared with 9.0 h per day [IQR 4.4–10.6] in the prone group (P 0.014). Nine
=
patients (23%) in the control group had pressure sores compared with two patients (6%) in the prone group (dif-
ference 18% (95% CI 2 to 33%); P 0.032). There were no other differences in secondary outcomes between
− − − =
groups.
Conclusions: The implemented protocol for awake prone positioning increased duration of prone positioning, but
did not reduce the rate of intubation in patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19 compared to
standard care.
Trial registration: ISRCTN54917435. Registered 15 June 2020 (https:// doi. org/ 10. 1186/ ISRCT N5491 7435).

_title: Awake prone positioning in patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COVID-19: the PROFLO multicenter randomized clinical trial
